facts.text: Franklin, New York, takes its name from Benjamin Franklin’s son or possibly his eldest son William, who held a large land patent in the area.
The village’s historic Stone Hall, rebuilt after a fire in 1856, remains a landmark of this era.
The Wilderstein mansion, a current historical site on the Hudson River, features both colonial and archaeological history dating from June 1853.
Montgomery's cottage still stands, although it was moved to 77 Livingston Street, where it houses the local Daughters of the American Revolution chapter.
Founded in 1835, the Delaware Literary Institute became a prestigious institution of higher learning.
European settlement in the area began in 1686 when Dutch settlers crossed the Hudson from Kingston and bought 2,200 acres from the local Sepasco tribe.
